skill.name: tools-ui
skill.description: Tool lifecycle UI components for React and Next.js applications. Use it to display tool calls, progress, approvals, results, and errors in agent interfaces.
skill.summary: Build React interfaces for agent tool status, approvals, and results.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged Markdown backticks, hardcoded URLs, and weak-crypto keywords, but review found no executable skill code or cryptographic implementation. The only real concern is supply-chain exposure from user-facing npx installation commands that fetch remote packages or registries.

low_findings.title: Remote installation commands require user trust
low_findings.description: The skill documents npx commands that fetch components or related skills from remote sources. This is a normal installation pattern, but users should review the referenced packages before running the commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present in Markdown examples and would contact remote package or skill sources if a user runs them. They are documentation examples, not automatically executed by the skill.

low_findings.title: Markdown backticks misclassified as shell execution
low_findings.description: The static external-command findings correspond to Markdown code fences and inline code formatting. No Ruby backtick execution or automatic command execution was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are fenced Markdown examples or inline code labels. There is no script file, Ruby code, or runtime path that executes the backtick content.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are documentation and component references
low_findings.description: The network findings are links to ui.inference.sh, cloud.inference.sh, and inference.sh documentation. No evidence found that the skill sends local data or secrets to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible Markdown links, an image reference, and an installation registry URL. The file contains no request code or data collection logic.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections are false positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto findings match descriptive text and field names, not a cryptographic algorithm implementation. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak crypto usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain metadata, table headings, or a tool description string. None of those lines implement or configure cryptography.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-facing npx remote install command
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick start instructs users to run npx with a remote registry URL. This pattern can introduce supply-chain risk if the remote source changes or is compromised.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The remote install command is explicit and reproducible. The risk is limited because it is documentation-only and requires intentional user action.

content.value_statement: Agent apps need clear displays for tool activity, approvals, and results. This skill provides React and Next.js guidance for adding tool lifecycle UI components.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install tool UI components from ui.inference.sh.
Documents component usage for tool calls, tool results, and tool approvals.
Explains supported tool states including pending, running, approval, success, and error.
Provides React examples for displaying lifecycle state from a tool object.
Shows how tool names map to common icons for search, file, write, delete, and mail actions.
limitations: The skill is documentation-only and does not include local component source files.
Installation depends on remote registries and packages referenced in the examples.
Examples assume a React or Next.js project using compatible component conventions.
The skill does not implement tool execution, permission checks, or backend approval logic.

use_cases.title: Add tool status to an agent app
use_cases.description: Display when an agent tool is pending, running, complete, or failed inside a React interface.

use_cases.title: Create approval gates for sensitive tools
use_cases.description: Show approval and denial controls before actions such as sending messages or calling external services.

use_cases.title: Standardize tool result displays
use_cases.description: Present tool outputs in a consistent format across search, file, email, and custom agent tools.

prompt_templates.title: Install the components
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup in an existing React project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add the tools-ui components to my React project and explain what files or commands I need to review first.

prompt_templates.title: Render a tool call
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to render a running tool call named search_web with visible arguments and status.

prompt_templates.title: Add approval handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a tool approval flow for a send_email tool, including approve and deny handlers in a Next.js component.

prompt_templates.title: Design a full lifecycle view
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a reusable ToolDisplay component that chooses approval, result, or running views from one tool state object.

output_examples.input: I need to show a running search tool in my agent UI.
output_examples.output: A ToolCall display is added with the search_web name, visible query arguments, and a running status indicator.

output_examples.input: I need human approval before an email tool runs.
output_examples.output: A ToolApproval view is added with approve and deny callbacks, so the user can decide before execution continues.

output_examples.input: I need one component that handles every tool state.
output_examples.output: A lifecycle display selects the approval view, result view, or active call view based on the current tool object.

best_practices: Review remote installation commands before running them in a project.
Keep approval logic on trusted application code, not only in the visual component.
Show tool arguments and results clearly so users can understand each action.
anti_patterns: Do not run remote npx commands without reviewing the source and package behavior.
Do not treat UI approval buttons as the only security boundary for sensitive tools.
Do not hide tool arguments when users need context to approve or deny an action.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you add React UI patterns for agent tool calls, approval states, progress, results, and errors.

faq.question: Does it execute tools for me?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on displaying tool lifecycle state and approval controls in the user interface.

faq.question: Can I use it with Next.js?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples are written for React and Next.js style imports and components.

faq.question: Does it support human approval flows?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation shows approval components with approve and deny callbacks.

faq.question: Is there any security concern?
faq.answer: The main concern is reviewing remote installation commands before running them in your project.

faq.question: Can this work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can help present tool activity in interfaces used with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
